macro_segments.title: Boats and Tumah: The debate between Chananya and the Rabbis

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the daf and the Mishnah on boats
Chananya's principle: objects must be carriable when full
Application to boats: the Jordan boat (sfinat hayarden) as a case study

macro_segments.title: The value of constant Torah study: teachings of Rav, Rabbi Yonatan, and Reish Lakish

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's teaching: never withhold yourself from the study hall
The hidden reasoning revealed: Rabbi Chanina ben Akavya's explanation of the Jordan boat
Rabbi Yonatan's perspective: learning Torah for its own sake, even unto death
Reish Lakish's synthesis: self-sacrifice and the establishment of Torah

macro_segments.title: Resumption of the boat discussion: Rava's formulation and the principle of carriability

micro_segments.title: Rava's statement: according to Chananya, even movement by oxen counts as carriability
The Mishnah on wagons: three types and their rules of tumah
First wagon type: covered wagon (cathedra) becomes tumei midras
Second wagon type: flatbed for goods—tumei from other sources but not midras
Third wagon type: stone-carrying wagon with large holes—completely tahor
The role of oxen in defining carriability for tumah

macro_segments.title: The Mishnah on three types of chests and their tumah status

micro_segments.title: First chest type: opening on the side—tumei midras
Second chest type: opening from top—tumei met but not midras
Third chest type: huge size—completely tahor

macro_segments.title: Pottery vessels and the exclusion from midras tumah

micro_segments.title: Tanna Kamma: pottery vessels cannot become midras
Rav Zevid's reading: pottery vessels excluded from midras but not from other tumot
Pottery boat and Rabbi Yosi's position: debate over categorical boat exclusion
Alternative reading: wooden vessels become tumei; boats remain tahor

macro_segments.title: Why pottery cannot become midras: the mikveh principle

micro_segments.title: Chizkiya's derivation: midras status requires purification capability in mikveh
Connection of mishkav to personal tumah status: impermanence principle

macro_segments.title: The mat (pifrach) and exceptional midras status despite non-receptacle form

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Ila's challenge: how can a mat become midras if it lacks mikveh purification?

macro_segments.title: Why pottery cannot become midras: the mikveh principle (continued)

micro_segments.title: The bat midras as a back-door entry to other tumot

macro_segments.title: The mat (pifrach) and exceptional midras status despite non-receptacle form (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chanina's answer: midras applies if material is in the category of mikveh-purifiable materials
Two competing verses reconciled: categories and their sufficiency

macro_segments.title: Rava's alternative explanation: pottery sealed vessels remain tahor

micro_segments.title: The sealed pottery vessel rule and its implications for midras status
Proof that pottery cannot be midras through the seal principle

macro_segments.title: Transition to Zra'im: the Mishnah on planted beds (arugah) with five seeds

micro_segments.title: Mishnah setup: six by six tefach bed with five different seed types
Derivation from scripture: how five types are permitted in one bed
Rashi's principle: kilayim requires visual separation, not nutritional separation
Direction and distance as separators in kilayim law
Rashi's view: kilayim of non-vine plants is rabbinic only

Sfinat hayarden (Jordan boat): paradigm case
Carriability principle (taltula yedei shevarim)
Beit kibbul (receptacle criterion for wooden vessels)
Midras (tumah of seats/lying surfaces)
Pottery (cheres) exclusion from tumah
Mikveh purification and mishkav status
Value of Torah study and constant engagement
Rav Yehuda / Rav: study hall attendance
Rabbi Yonatan: learning Torah unto death
Reish Lakish: self-sacrifice and Torah establishment
Rabbi Chanina ben Akavya: explanation of boat tumah
Rava: formulation of carriability rule
Wagons and their three types (cathedra, flatbed, stone-carrier)
Chests and their three types (side-opening, top-opening, huge)
Rabbi Yosi: boat exclusion from tumah
Rav Pappa: challenge to pottery midras reasoning
Chizkiya: mikveh purification as criterion for midras
Rabbi Ila: challenge to mat as midras
Tosafot: disagreement with Rashi on wood vessel purification
Mat (pifrach) and exceptional midras category
Pachim ktanim: little vessels tahor by zav, tamei by met
Kilayim: mixing of seeds prohibition
Arugah: six by six tefach garden bed with five seeds
Rashi: visual separation criterion for kilayim (not nutritional)
Kilay haKerem vs. Kilay Zra'im: biblical status dispute
